source_title: Constituent Assembly of India
fact_summary: The Constituent Assembly first met in New Delhi on 9 December 1946 in Constitution Hall, now known as the Central Hall of Parliament House. Parliament records that the Assembly took two years, eleven months and seventeen days to complete its drafting task, holding eleven sessions over 165 days, of which 114 days were spent considering the Draft Constitution.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Parliament of India Constituent Assembly history page; stable institutional dates only.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: The Preamble declares India a sovereign, socialist, secular, democratic republic. It resolves to secure social, economic and political justice; liberty of thought, expression, belief, faith and worship; equality of status and opportunity; and fraternity that assures individual dignity and the unity and integrity of the nation.
verification_notes: Manually transcribed and summarized from the Preamble in the Legislative Department's Constitution current to 1 May 2026.

source_title: The Citizenship Act, 1955
fact_summary: The Citizenship Act provides acquisition routes through birth, descent, registration, naturalisation and incorporation of territory in sections 3 through 7. It separately governs loss of citizenship through renunciation, termination on voluntary acquisition of foreign citizenship, and deprivation by Central Government order in sections 8 through 10.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against sections 3-10 of the Ministry of Home Affairs consolidated Citizenship Act.

source_title: The Citizenship (Amendment) Act, 2019
fact_summary: The Citizenship (Amendment) Act, 2019 amended the 1955 Act, including its treatment of specified Hindu, Sikh, Buddhist, Jain, Parsi and Christian migrants from Afghanistan, Bangladesh and Pakistan who entered India on or before 31 December 2014, and inserted section 6B governing citizenship by registration or naturalisation for the covered class.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the enacted 2019 amendment text; the summary states the statutory scope without adding policy interpretation.

source_title: Overseas Citizen of India (OCI) Cardholder
fact_summary: OCI is a statutory cardholder status under sections 7A through 7D of the Citizenship Act rather than dual Indian citizenship. The Ministry's materials cover eligibility, registration, notified rights and restrictions, and the circumstances in which an OCI registration may be cancelled; political rights reserved to citizens are not conferred merely by OCI registration.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Ministry of Home Affairs OCI page and its linked statutory guidance.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: Article 75 provides that the President appoints the Prime Minister and appoints other ministers on the Prime Minister's advice. Under articles 74 and 78, the Prime Minister heads the Council that aids and advises the President and must communicate Council decisions and requested information about Union administration and legislative proposals to the President.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against articles 74, 75 and 78 of the current Constitution.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: Article 74 establishes a Council of Ministers with the Prime Minister at its head to aid and advise the President. Article 75 limits the total number of ministers, including the Prime Minister, to fifteen per cent of Lok Sabha membership. Article 352 uses Cabinet to mean the Council consisting of the Prime Minister and other ministers of Cabinet rank.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the constitutional provisions distinguishing the Council of Ministers and the Cabinet.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: Article 75 says ministers hold office during the President's pleasure and makes the Council of Ministers collectively responsible to the House of the People. A minister who is not a member of either House of Parliament for six consecutive months ceases to be a minister, while the Third Schedule prescribes separate oaths of office and secrecy.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against article 75 and the Third Schedule; collective and office-tenure rules are kept distinct.

source_title: Functions of the Cabinet Secretariat
fact_summary: The Cabinet Secretariat administers the Government of India Transaction of Business Rules and Allocation of Business Rules framed under article 77(3). It supplies secretarial assistance to the Cabinet and its committees, coordinates inter-ministerial business, and supports the prescribed process through which government decisions are considered and recorded.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Cabinet Secretariat's official functions page; no current office-holder names are included.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: Article 76 requires the President to appoint as Attorney-General for India a person qualified to be a Supreme Court judge. The Attorney-General advises the Government of India on referred legal matters, performs assigned legal duties, has a right of audience in all courts in India, and holds office during the President's pleasure.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against article 76; the constitutional office is kept separate from Cabinet Secretariat functions.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: Articles 153-162 establish the Governor's office and State executive power. The President appoints the Governor, who normally holds office for five years subject to the President's pleasure. State executive action is formally expressed in the Governor's name, while constitutional functions are ordinarily exercised on the aid and advice framework in article 163.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against articles 153-163; the summary does not overstate discretionary power.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: Articles 163 and 164 provide for a Council of Ministers headed by the Chief Minister to aid and advise the Governor. The Governor appoints the Chief Minister and appoints other ministers on the Chief Minister's advice. The State Council is collectively responsible to the Legislative Assembly, and article 167 specifies the Chief Minister's duties toward the Governor.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against articles 163-167 of the current Constitution.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: Article 168 provides either a unicameral State Legislature consisting of the Governor and Legislative Assembly or a bicameral legislature also containing a Legislative Council. Articles 170 and 171 govern composition, article 172 generally gives an Assembly a five-year term unless sooner dissolved, and the Council is a continuing body with staggered retirement.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against articles 168-172; Assembly and Council tenure rules are stated separately.

micro_topic_name: রাজ্য বিল, বাজেট ও অ্যাডভোকেট জেনারেল
source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: Articles 196-201 regulate State bills, including passage, assent, return of a non-Money Bill and reservation for the President. Articles 202-207 govern the annual financial statement, grants, appropriation and financial bills. Article 165 creates the Advocate-General for a State, appointed by the Governor from persons qualified to be High Court judges.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against articles 165 and 196-207; three related concepts are sourced from their exact constitutional parts.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: Part XI distributes legislative and administrative relations between the Union and States, read with the Union, State and Concurrent Lists in the Seventh Schedule. Part XII addresses financial relations, including taxation and distribution provisions, grants, the Finance Commission, borrowing, property and government contracts.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against Parts XI-XII and the Seventh Schedule; the summary stays at the stable structural level.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: Article 263 allows the President to establish an Inter-State Council when public interests would be served, with duties that may include inquiry into and advice on inter-State disputes and discussion or recommendation on subjects of common interest. Article 262 separately authorises Parliament to provide adjudication of inter-State river or river-valley disputes.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against articles 262-263; Council advice and statutory water-dispute adjudication are not conflated.

source_title: The Inter-State River Water Disputes Act, 1956
fact_summary: The Inter-State River Water Disputes Act, 1956 provides a statutory process for adjudicating disputes concerning waters of inter-State rivers and river valleys. A State may request Central Government action; when negotiation cannot settle the dispute, the Act provides for referral to a Water Disputes Tribunal and governs publication and effect of its decision.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Ministry of Jal Shakti consolidated Act; only the stable adjudication framework is summarized.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: Article 352 permits a national emergency proclamation when India's security or part of it is threatened by war, external aggression or armed rebellion, subject to written Cabinet communication and parliamentary approval. Article 356 concerns failure of constitutional machinery in a State and permits a proclamation with distinct parliamentary approval and duration rules.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against articles 352 and 356; grounds and safeguards for the two proclamations remain distinct.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: Article 360 permits a proclamation if the President is satisfied that India's financial stability or credit, or that of any part of its territory, is threatened. It requires parliamentary approval within two months and can support directions to States on financial propriety, including specified salary reductions and reservation of State Money Bills for presidential consideration.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against article 360; no claim is made that a financial emergency has actually been proclaimed.

source_title: 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992
fact_summary: The Seventy-third Amendment added Part IX on Panchayats and the Eleventh Schedule. Its constitutional framework covers Gram Sabhas, Panchayats at village, intermediate and district levels subject to stated exceptions, direct elections, reservations, five-year duration, powers and responsibilities, finances, State Finance Commissions and State Election Commissions.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Ministry of Panchayati Raj's accessible amendment text and Part IX.

source_title: The Constitution (74th Amendment) Act: Salient Features
fact_summary: The Seventy-fourth Amendment added Part IXA and the Twelfth Schedule for urban local government. The official summary identifies constitution and composition of municipalities, ward committees, seat reservation, fixed duration, municipal powers and responsibilities, State Election Commissions and State Finance Commissions as core features of the framework.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs constitutional-amendment summary.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: Articles 243K and 243ZA vest superintendence, direction and control of Panchayat and municipal electoral-roll preparation and elections in the State Election Commission, subject to their respective provisions. Articles 243I and 243Y require periodic State Finance Commissions to review local-body finances and make recommendations to the Governor.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against articles 243I, 243K, 243Y and 243ZA; electoral and financial institutions are distinguished.

source_title: State Panchayati Raj Acts, Rules and Regulations
fact_summary: The Ministry of Panchayati Raj's State legislation index identifies the West Bengal Panchayat Act, 1973 as the State's governing Panchayati Raj enactment and records the West Bengal Panchayat (Amendment) Act, 2012 in connection with fifty per cent reservation for women in Panchayati Raj Institutions.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Ministry's State-by-State Panchayati Raj legislation index.

source_title: West Bengal Urban Development and Municipal Affairs: Acts and Rules
fact_summary: The West Bengal Urban Development and Municipal Affairs Department's official index lists the West Bengal Municipal Act, 1993, the West Bengal Municipal Elections Act, 1994, the West Bengal Municipal Corporation Act, 2006, and corporation-specific laws including the Kolkata and Howrah Municipal Corporation Acts among the State's urban local-government enactments.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the State department's Acts and Rules index; only listed enactments are summarized.

source_title: West Bengal State Election Commission
fact_summary: The West Bengal State Election Commission states that it conducts the local election process for Panchayats and Municipal Bodies under the constitutional and State-law framework. It distinguishes that role from the Election Commission of India's responsibility for electoral-roll and voter-card matters outside the State Commission's local-body remit.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the official State Election Commission homepage and its institutional description.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: Article 368 allows Parliament to exercise constituent power through a Bill passed in each House by a majority of the total membership and at least two-thirds of members present and voting. Amendments affecting specified federal provisions also require ratification by at least half of State Legislatures before the Bill is presented for presidential assent.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against article 368; ordinary-law changes outside that procedure are not mixed into the summary.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: The Constitution's text and footnotes record major changes including the Forty-second Amendment's Preamble terms, Forty-fourth Amendment emergency safeguards, Fifty-second Amendment Tenth Schedule, Sixty-first Amendment voting age of eighteen, Seventy-third and Seventy-fourth local-government parts, One Hundred and First GST framework and One Hundred and Sixth women's-reservation provisions.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the current constitutional text and its amendment footnotes; the list is illustrative rather than exhaustive.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: The Constitution contains twelve Schedules. They cover States and Union territories; constitutional emoluments; oaths; Rajya Sabha seat allocation; Scheduled Areas and tribal administration; specified northeastern tribal areas; legislative lists; recognised languages; listed laws; anti-defection; Panchayat subjects; and municipal subjects, respectively.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the headings and contents of all twelve Schedules in the current Constitution.

source_title: The Constitution of India [As on 1st May, 2026]
fact_summary: Article 324 vests superintendence, direction and control of electoral rolls and elections to Parliament, State Legislatures, and the offices of President and Vice-President in the Election Commission. The Tenth Schedule sets disqualification rules for defection, including voluntary relinquishment of party membership and voting or abstaining contrary to authorised direction, subject to its exceptions.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against article 324 and the Tenth Schedule; statutory election-law details are sourced separately.

source_title: Election Laws
fact_summary: The Election Commission's official election-law collection links the Representation of the People Acts of 1950 and 1951. The 1950 Act principally covers allocation of seats and electoral rolls, while the 1951 Act governs conduct of elections, qualifications and disqualifications, political-party registration, corrupt practices, election offences and election disputes.
verification_notes: Manually reviewed against the Election Commission's official election-law index and the linked central Acts.
